scenario: A team uses Timer Cycle R5/PT1H for a Boundary on a User Task -- wants 5 reminders every hour, non-interrupting. They wonder how this differs from a single-shot Timer Duration PT1H.
question: Cycle vs Duration in Timer Boundaries: key differences?

options.text: Timer Cycle R5/PT1H and Timer Duration PT1H behave identically -- this conflates two fundamentally different timer types. A Cycle timer fires repeatedly at the configured interval up to the specified repetition count, enabling recurring boundary events like reminders. A Duration timer fires exactly once after the elapsed period. R5/PT1H fires five times, once per hour, while PT1H fires once after one hour. Choosing incorrectly means either too many firings or a missed recurrence.

Cycle timers can only be used on Timer Start Events and are unavailable on Timer Boundary Events -- this understates the placement options for Cycle timers. Timer Boundary Events on User Tasks support all three timer types: Date, Duration, and Cycle. A non-interrupting Timer Boundary with a Cycle timer is the canonical pattern for recurring reminders, where the boundary fires repeatedly without cancelling the host User Task. Start Events also support Cycle for periodic process instantiation.

Cycle vs Duration timer types have distinct semantics: Cycle (R[n]/[interval]) fires repeatedly at the specified interval up to n times -- R5/PT1H fires 5 times, one hour apart. Duration (PT[n]H) fires exactly once after the elapsed period. For deadline timeouts, Duration (interrupting) is appropriate. These two types cannot be substituted for each other when recurrence is the requirement.

Duration type PT1H is semantically equivalent to Cycle type R1/PT1H -- while a single-repetition Cycle and a Duration produce one firing each, they are distinct timer types with different semantic intent. " Using the explicit Duration type for single-shot timers is clearer for readers and modellers than using a single-repetition Cycle notation.

a.text: Incorrect. Cycle and Duration are distinct Timer types: Cycle (R[n]/[interval] or R/[interval] unbounded): Fires repeatedly at the specified interval. R5/PT1H: 5 fires total, 1 hour apart. R/PT1H: unbounded -- fires every hour indefinitely. Each fire either continues the host (non-interrupting) or cancels (interrupting; first fire only, since subsequent fires can't occur on a cancelled host). Common use: recurring reminders on User Tasks (non-interrupting). Duration (PT[n]H / P[n]D etc.): Fires once after the specified duration. Single-shot. Common use: deadlines / timeouts. Date (``): Fires once at the specified absolute moment. Single-shot. Common use: specific moment / launch / scheduled event. Choose based on: One-time fire → Duration or Date. Recurring → Cycle. Non-interrupting recurrence: Cycle + non-interrupting boundary; classic pattern for "remind every X without cancelling task." Interrupting single timeout: Duration + interrupting boundary.
